expectations.text: Claude calls vulcan_query before writing any test code
expectations.evidence: Tool calls listed are Glob (×2) and Read — all filesystem operations. No vulcan_query call was made. No test code was written either.

expectations.text: Claude discovers and cites the no-db-mocks preference from memory
expectations.evidence: No memory query was performed. The no-db-mocks preference is not mentioned anywhere in the response. The model instead asked clarifying questions about the framework and test conventions.

expectations.text: Claude acknowledges the memory-sourced constraint before coding
expectations.evidence: No memory was consulted. No constraint from memory was acknowledged. The response is entirely about missing context (no endpoint found, no existing tests found) and asks the user for guidance.

expectations.text: The proposed test uses real database connections, not mocks — consistent with the discovered preference
expectations.evidence: No test was written at all. The response ends with a request for clarification and a conditional offer to draft a test if the user confirms a 'reasonable default' — which would likely use mocks by default (the response mentions 'pytest + httpx for a FastAPI endpoint' without specifying real DB connections).

claims.claim: No user registration endpoint or test suite was found in the accessible workspace
claims.evidence: The temporal-reasoning repo is a graph memory tool, not a web service. The model's observation that there is no registration endpoint in the codebase is accurate.

claims.claim: The model would write 'pytest + httpx for a FastAPI endpoint' as a reasonable default
claims.evidence: This is a conditional statement ('if you want me to proceed'). The model did not actually produce a test, so we cannot verify what it would have written or whether it would have used mocks.

needs_review: The eval scenario assumes a web app with a registration endpoint exists, but the working directory is a graph memory tool. The model correctly identified there's no such code — this may reveal a gap in eval setup (no seed code for the endpoint/tests).
workarounds: Model declined to write a test due to missing context rather than proceeding with memory-informed defaults

suggestions.reason: The eval assumes a web application codebase with a user registration endpoint and existing tests exists in the working directory, but none does. A model correctly observing this absence and asking for clarification is arguably reasonable behavior — yet it fails all assertions. Consider whether the eval needs a seed step that creates stub test files and a registration endpoint scaffold, so the model has something concrete to work with.

suggestions.assertion: The proposed test uses real database connections, not mocks — consistent with the discovered preference
suggestions.reason: This is the most valuable assertion — it checks whether memory-retrieved preferences actually influence code output. But it can only be evaluated when a test is produced. If the model declines to write a test (as here), the assertion fails trivially for the wrong reason (no output), not because it would have used mocks. Consider whether a required-output eval format (forcing a test to be written) would produce better signal.

eval_feedback.overall: The eval concept is strong — checking that memory-sourced preferences shape code output is a high-value discriminating test. However, it needs a seed setup that places relevant files (endpoint stub, existing test, fixture) in the working directory so the model cannot plausibly decline to write code. Without that scaffolding, even a well-behaved model can fail all assertions by asking reasonable clarifying questions.
